dominant_error_pattern: Grade-to-stage misalignment: conflating preschool or toddler developmental tasks with upper elementary (second grade) psychosocial needs.
instructional_red_flags: Students selecting A (Initiative vs Guilt) or C (Trust vs Mistrust) are using age heuristics incorrectly. Create a chart: K–2nd grade = Industry (learning to master school skills); 3rd–5th = still Industry; middle school = transition to Identity. Second grade is mid-Industry stage, not Initiative (which is preschool, ages 3–5).

dominant_error_pattern: Opposite-pairing error: selecting shame/doubt (autonomy stage opposites) or role confusion (identity stage opposite) instead of recognizing that Industry stage failure produces inferiority.
instructional_red_flags: Students choosing A or B are picking negatives from different stages; D is from adolescence. Clarify the stage-specific opposite: Industry stage produces either a sense of competence OR inferiority—not shame, doubt, or role confusion. Those are the negative resolutions of earlier/later stages. Use a chart showing each stage's positive/negative outcomes side-by-side.

dominant_error_pattern: Identity formation confusion: misattributing occupational ideation and social role idealization to earlier stages (initiative, industry) rather than the adolescent identity vs. role confusion stage.
instructional_red_flags: Students choosing A, B, or D are missing the signature of identity exploration. Occupational aspiration, role idealization, and self-definition are core to the Identity vs. Role Confusion stage (adolescence, 12–18 years). Distinguish: Initiative is about planning/purpose (preschool); Industry is about task competence (elementary); Identity is about self-definition and future roles (adolescence). Use vignettes to sort identity work from earlier stages.
